blocks:
Ayahs

قٓ ۚ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ٱلْمَجِيدِ (١)
Qaf. By the glorious Quran. (1)
بَلْ عَجِبُوٓا۟ أَن جَآءَهُم مُّنذِرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَقَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا شَىْءٌ عَجِيبٌ (٢)
Indeed they consider it odd that a warner from among themselves should have come to them. So the faithless say, ‘This is an odd thing.’ (2)
أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا ۖ ذَٰلِكَ رَجْعٌۢ بَعِيدٌ (٣)
‘What! When we are dead and have become dust, [shall we be raised again]? That is a far-fetched return!’ (3)
قَدْ عَلِمْنَا مَا تَنقُصُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَعِندَنَا كِتَـٰبٌ حَفِيظٌۢ (٤)
We know what the earth diminishes from them, and with Us is a preserving Book. (4)
بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِىٓ أَمْرٍ مَّرِيجٍ (٥)
Indeed, they denied the truth when it came to them; so they are now in a perplexed state of affairs. (5)

Commentary

Stubborn Deniers are Wandering in Their Affairs!

Here, at the beginning of this Surah, we once again encounter some of the "Abbreviated Letters" (al-huruf al-muqatta'ah), specifically the letter "Qaf." As we have previously mentioned, one significant interpretation of these "Abbreviated Letters" is that the Quran, with all its grandeur, is composed of simple elements like the letters of the alphabet. This demonstrates that the Creator and Revealer of the Holy Quran possesses infinite knowledge and power, having created such a sublime composition from such simple tools.

Of course, there are other interpretations for the "Abbreviated Letters" as well, which you may study at the beginning of Surahs Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, Al-A'raf, and the "Ha-Mim" Surahs.

Some commentators have also considered "Qaf" to be an allusion to some of the Names of Allah (such as al-Qadir [The Powerful] and al-Qayyum [The Self-Subsisting]).

In many commentaries, it is also mentioned that "Qaf" is the name of a mighty mountain that encompasses the Earth. As for which mountain this is that surrounds the Earth or the entire universe, and what is meant by it, this is not the place for that discussion. What we deem necessary to mention here is that it seems very unlikely that "Qaf" in this Surah refers to "Mount Qaf," because not only does it lack any proportion to the themes of the Surah, but the letter "Qaf" here is just like the other abbreviated letters appearing at the start of Quranic Surahs. Furthermore, if "Mount Qaf" were intended, it should have been accompanied by the "Waw" of oath-taking, such as "Wa al-Tur" (By the Mount) and the like; mentioning a single word without a subject and predicate or an oath-particle carries no meaning.

Beyond all this, the "orthography" (rasm al-khat) of all copies of the Quran is that "Qaf" is written as a single letter (ق), whereas "Mount Qaf" is written out as a full word (قاف).

Among the matters testifying that the mention of this letter from the abbreviated letters is to express the greatness of the Quran is that immediately following it, He swears by the Glorious Quran, saying: "By the Glorious Quran" (وَ الْقُرْآنِ الْمَجِيدِ).

"Majid" is derived from the root "majd," meaning "extensive nobility." Since the Quran possesses infinite greatness and nobility, the word "Majid" is deserving of it from every aspect: its outward appearance is beautiful, its content is magnificent, its commands are sublime, and its programs are educational and life-giving.

Regarding the purpose for which this oath was mentioned—and what is termed the "muqsam lahu" (the subject of the oath)—commentators have offered many possibilities. However, considering the subsequent verses, it appears that the answer to the oath is the issue of the "Prophethood" of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله or the "Resurrection" of human beings after death. [^1]

[^1]: The implied meaning is: "By the Glorious Quran, indeed you are the Messenger of Allah"—or—"Indeed the Resurrection is true."

Then, it proceeds to state some of the baseless objections of the Arab disbelievers and polytheists, highlighting two of them:

First, it says: "But they wonder that there has come to them a warner from among themselves, and the disbelievers say, 'This is an amazing thing!'" (بَلْ عَجِبُوا أَنْ جاءَهُمْ مُنْذِرٌ مِنْهُمْ فَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا شَيْ‌ءٌ عَجِيبٌ).

This is an objection that the Quran has mentioned and answered many times, and its repetition shows that it was one of the primary objections of the disbelievers which they constantly repeated.

They raised this objection not only against the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله but against other prophets as well. Sometimes they would say: "You are but human beings like us, who wish to avert us from what our fathers used to worship!" (Ibrahim: 10).

And sometimes they said: "This is but a human being like yourselves; he eats of what you eat and drinks of what you drink!" (Al-Mu'minun: 33).

And sometimes they added: "Why was there not sent down to him an angel to be a warner with him?" (Al-Furqan: 7).

But all of these were excuses to avoid submitting to the Truth.

In the verses under discussion, the Quran does not provide an answer to this objection because it has answered it many times before: that if We had sent an angel, We would have made him in the form of a human. That is, the leader and guide of humanity can only be a human, so that he may be aware of all their pains, needs, inclinations, desires, and life issues, and on the other hand, serve as a practical role model for them. Otherwise, they would say: "If he were of our kind, he would never remain pure and untainted," for as the poet says: If the judge sits with us, he will wave his hand [in dismissal], If the inspector drinks, he excuses the drunkard! Therefore, his programs would only be suitable for himself, not for humanity!

After this objection to the mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and how he could be of human kind, they had another objection regarding the content of his call, pointing to a matter that was strange and bizarre to them from every perspective. They said: "When we have died and have become dust, [shall we be resurrected]? That is a far-fetched return!" (أَ إِذا مِتْنا وَ كُنَّا تُراباً ذلِكَ رَجْعٌ بَعِيدٌ) [^1]. In any case, they considered the return to life a matter far from reason; rather, they sometimes deemed it impossible and took the claim of it as evidence of the speaker's insanity! As we read in verses 7 and 8 of Surah Saba: "The disbelievers say, 'Shall we direct you to a man who informs you that when you have been disintegrated in total disintegration, you will [then] be in a new creation? Has he invented a lie about Allah or is there a madness in him?'"

It is not only here that they raised this objection to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله; they said it many times, heard the answer many times, and yet repeated it out of stubbornness.

[^1]: The answer to "idha" (when) is omitted and understood from the following sentence; the implied meaning is: "When we have died and become dust, shall we return and be restored to life? That is a far-fetched return."

In any case, the Holy Quran responds to them here in several ways:

First: It points to the infinite knowledge of God, saying: "We know what the earth diminishes of them, and with Us is a Book that preserves [everything]" (قَدْ عَلِمْنا ما تَنْقُصُ الْأَرْضُ مِنْهُمْ وَ عِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ).

If your problem and objection is because human bones decay, and his flesh becomes dust and becomes part of the earth, and particles of it turn into vapor and gases scattered in the air—who can collect them? And who is even aware of them?

The answer is clear: The God whose knowledge encompasses all things knows all these particles, and when necessary, He collects them all, just as scattered iron filings in a heap of dust can be collected with a magnet. Collecting the scattered particles of every human is even easier for God than that.

And if their objection is: who keeps the account of human deeds for the Hereafter and Resurrection? The answer is that all of these are recorded in the "Preserved Tablet" (Lawh Mahfuz), and fundamentally, nothing is lost in this universe; even your deeds remain, though they may change form.

"Kitabun Hafiz" means a book that preserves the deeds of all humans and others, referring to the "Preserved Tablet," the explanation of which we provided under verse 39 of Surah Ar-Ra'd (Volume 10, page 241).

Then it proceeds to another answer which is more psychological in nature, saying:

"But they denied the truth when it came to them" (بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ).

That is, they have consciously denied the truth; otherwise, there is no dust upon the face of truth, and as will come in the following verses, they repeatedly witness the scene of resurrection with their own eyes in this world, leaving no room for doubt.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, it adds: "So they are in a confused state" (فَهُمْ فِي أَمْرٍ مَرِيجٍ). Since they have set out to deny, they constantly speak contradictions, are bewildered in their work, and are caught in confused matters.

Sometimes they call the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله a madman, sometimes a soothsayer, and sometimes a poet. Sometimes they say: his words are "fables of the ancients." Sometimes they say: "A human being teaches him!" Sometimes they consider the influence of his words a type of "sorcery." And sometimes they claim: "We too can produce the like of it!" These scattered sayings show that they have perceived the truth but are seeking excuses, and therefore never stand by a single word.

"Marij" is from the root "maraj" (on the pattern of haraj), meaning a "confused, disturbed, and ambiguous" matter. Hence, a land where various and abundant plants have grown is called "marj" (a pasture).

Ayahs

أَفَلَمْ يَنظُرُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَوْقَهُمْ كَيْفَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا وَزَيَّنَّـٰهَا وَمَا لَهَا مِن فُرُوجٍ (٦)
Have they not, then, observed the heaven above them, how We have built it and adorned it, and that there are no cracks in it? (6)
وَٱلْأَرْضَ مَدَدْنَـٰهَا وَأَلْقَيْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ (٧)
And We spread out the earth, and cast in it firm mountains, and caused every delightful kind [of vegetation] to grow in it. (7)
تَبْصِرَةً وَذِكْرَىٰ لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ (٨)
[In this there is] an insight and admonition for every penitent servant. (8)
وَنَزَّلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً مُّبَـٰرَكًا فَأَنۢبَتْنَا بِهِۦ جَنَّـٰتٍ وَحَبَّ ٱلْحَصِيدِ (٩)
We send down from the sky salubrious water, with which We grow gardens and the grain which is harvested, (9)
وَٱلنَّخْلَ بَاسِقَـٰتٍ لَّهَا طَلْعٌ نَّضِيدٌ (١٠)
and tall date palms with regularly set spathes, (10)
رِّزْقًا لِّلْعِبَادِ ۖ وَأَحْيَيْنَا بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْخُرُوجُ (١١)
as a provision for Our servants, and with it We revive a dead country. Likewise will be the rising [from the dead]. (11)

Commentary

Look at the Sky for a Moment!

These verses continue the discussion on the "proofs of the Resurrection," sometimes drawing support from the "infinite power of the Truth (Allah)" and at other times from the "existence of scenes of the Resurrection within this very world."

First, it draws the attention of the deniers to the creation of the heavens, saying: "Have they not looked at the heaven above them, how We structured it and adorned it [with stars], and there are no rifts in it?" (أَ فَلَمْ يَنْظُرُوا إِلَى السَّماءِ فَوْقَهُمْ كَيْفَ بَنَيْناها وَ زَيَّنَّاها وَ ما لَها مِنْ فُرُوجٍ).

The meaning of "looking" here is a gaze accompanied by reflection and contemplation, which acquaints man with the magnificent power of the Creator of this vast heaven and its wonders—which possesses both staggering grandeur, abundant beauty, and firmness, order, and calculation.

The phrase وَ ما لَها مِنْ فُرُوجٍ (there are no rifts in it) either means the absence of any defect, flaw, or disproportion, as some commentators have stated, or it refers to the absence of rifts specifically in the sky that surrounds the Earth, known as the Earth's atmosphere. According to the Quran, it is a "protected roof" (Al-Anbiya, 32) that blocks the path of celestial rocks (meteors) which constantly rush toward the Earth at staggering speeds, burning them and turning them to ash before they reach the Earth's surface, and it also prevents harmful cosmic rays.

Otherwise, "heaven" in the sense of the location of the stars is a vacuum in which these spheres float.

There is also a third possibility here: that the above sentence refers to the theory of the existence of "ether" (aether). According to this theory, the entire universe and the spaces between stars are filled with a colorless and weightless substance called "ether" which carries light waves and transmits them from one point to another. According to this theory, there is no rift or gap in the entire universe of creation, and the planets and fixed stars are immersed in "ether."

Of course, these three interpretations are not mutually exclusive, although the third interpretation, which relies on the ether hypothesis, is not definitive, as the subject of ether has not yet been conclusively proven by scientists.

Then, addressing the greatness of the creation of the Earth, it adds: "And the earth—We spread it out and cast therein firmly set mountains and made grow therein [something] of every beautiful kind [in pairs]" (وَ الْأَرْضَ مَدَدْناها وَ أَلْقَيْنا فِيها رَواسِيَ وَ أَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ بَهِيجٍ).

Indeed, the creation of the Earth on one hand, its expansion (emerging from under the water) on the other, the emergence of mountains whose roots are interconnected and protect the Earth like armor against internal and external pressures and the tides resulting from the gravity of the moon and sun on the third hand, and the emergence of various types of plants with all those wonders and beauties on the fourth hand—all are evidence of His infinite power. [^1] The expression مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ (of every pair) refers to the issue of sexuality in the plant kingdom, which at the time of the revelation of these verses had never been discovered as a general principle, and only after centuries did human science and knowledge lift the veil from it; or it means the various classes and types of plants, as the diversity in the plant kingdom is extraordinarily vast and astonishing.

In the next verse, it draws a conclusion, saying: "All these We created for the insight and reminder of every servant who turns [to Us]" and wishes to perceive the truth (تَبْصِرَةً وَ ذِكْرى‌ لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُنِيبٍ). [^2] Indeed, He who has the power to create the heavens with all that grandeur and beauty, and the Earth with all this bounty, beauty, order, and calculation—how can He not clothe the dead once again in the garment of life and establish a Resurrection? Is this staggering, great power not a clear proof of the possibility of the Resurrection?

[^1]: Regarding the benefits of the creation of mountains, as well as the expansion of the Earth and sexuality in the plant kingdom, we had a detailed discussion in Surah Ar-Ra'd, Volume 10, page 113 onwards (under verse 3).

[^2]: "Tabsiratan" can be a "purpose object" (maf'ul lahu) or an "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq), but the first possibility is more appropriate. "Zikra" is also coordinated with it and conveys the same meaning.

In the next verse, He lays the foundation for another argument and says: "And We have sent down from the sky blessed water and made grow thereby gardens and grain from the harvest" (وَ نَزَّلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً مُبارَكاً فَأَنْبَتْنا بِهِ جَنَّاتٍ وَ حَبَّ الْحَصِيدِ).

"Jannat" here refers to fruit orchards, and "habb al-hasid" (grains for harvest) refers to cereals such as barley, wheat, and the like, which constitute the primary food sources for humans.

Then He adds: "And lofty palm trees having fruit arranged in layers" (وَ النَّخْلَ باسِقاتٍ لَها طَلْعٌ نَضِيدٌ). "Basigat" is the plural of "basiqah," meaning tall and lofty. "Tal'" refers to the fruit of the date palm at the beginning of its appearance, and "nadid" means dense and layered. Especially the cluster of the date palm, when it is inside its sheath, is perfectly stacked and dense upon one another, and when it emerges from the sheath, it is very amazing.

In the end, He says: "All these We created as provision for the servants, and We have given life thereby to a dead land. Thus is the resurrection [from the graves]!" (رِزْقاً لِلْعِبادِ وَ أَحيَينا بِهِ بَلدَةً مَيتاً كَذلِكَ الخُرُوجُ) [^1]. In this way, while reminding the servants of His great blessings and stimulating

[^1]: This matter has also been discussed in other verses (refer to Volume 18 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 190 onwards under verse 9 of Fatir, and page 485 of the same Volume 18 under the final verses of Surah Ya-Sin).

their sense of gratitude on the path of knowing Him, He reminds them that you see an example of the Resurrection every year before your eyes in this very world. Dead, dry lands, devoid of any signs of life, are set into motion by the falling of raindrops and raise the clamor of resurrection; from every corner, a plant grows and proclaims, "He is One, without partner!" This great movement and drive toward life in the plant kingdom expresses the reality that the Creator of the universe can grant life to dead beings once again, for the "occurrence" of something is the strongest proof of its "possibility."

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلرَّسِّ وَثَمُودُ (١٢)
The people of Noah denied before them, and [so did] the people of Rass and Thamud, (12)
وَعَادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ وَإِخْوَٰنُ لُوطٍ (١٣)
and ‘Ad, Pharaoh, and the brethren of Lot, (13)
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَيْكَةِ وَقَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ ۚ كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ (١٤)
and the inhabitants of Aykah and the people of Tubbac. Each [of them] impugned the apostles, and so My threat became due [against them]. (14)
أَفَعَيِينَا بِٱلْخَلْقِ ٱلْأَوَّلِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِى لَبْسٍ مِّنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (١٥)
Have We been exhausted by the first creation? No, they are in doubt about a new creation. (15)

Commentary

You Are Not the Only One Facing Enmity!

These verses continue the discussions regarding the Resurrection from various perspectives.

First, to comfort the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: You are not the only one whom this group of disbelievers has denied, nor is the content of your call—especially regarding the Resurrection—unique in being rejected; "Before them, the people of Noah and the companions of the Rass and Thamud also denied [their messengers]" (كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَ أَصْحابُ الرَّسِّ وَ ثَمُودُ).

The "People of Thamud" were the people of Salih عليه السلام, the great Prophet of God, who lived in the land of "al-Hijr" in northern Hijaz. Regarding the "Companions of the Rass" (Ashab al-Rass), there is discussion among commentators. Many believe they were a tribe living in the land of "Yamamah" who had a prophet named "Hanzhalah," whom they denied and eventually threw into a well (note that one of the meanings of "Rass" is a well, and another meaning is a slight trace remaining of something, as only few memories of this people remain in history).

Others consider them to be the people of Shu'ayb عليه السلام because they possessed many water wells; however, given that the "Companions of the Thicket" (Ashab al-Aykah) mentioned in the following verses refers to the people of Shu'ayb, this possibility is dismissed.

Some also consider them to be remnants of the people of Thamud, but since "Thamud" is mentioned separately in the verses under discussion, this meaning also seems unlikely.

Therefore, the first interpretation, which enjoys fame among commentators, is the most appropriate.

Then He adds: "And [so did] 'Ad and Pharaoh and the brothers of Lot" (وَ عادٌ وَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَ إِخْوانُ لُوطٍ). The meaning of the "brothers of Lot" is the people of Lot, as the Quran refers to these great Prophets as brothers [to their people].

"And also the Companions of the Thicket and the people of Tubba'" (وَ أَصْحابُ الْأَيْكَةِ وَ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ).

"Aykah" means abundant and tangled trees, or in other words, a forest-like area. The "Companions of the Thicket" were a group from the people of Shu'ayb who lived outside the city of "Madyan," in a city that had many trees [1]. The "People of Tubba'" refers to a group of people from Yemen, as "Tubba'" was a title for the kings of Yemen because the people followed (taba'iyyat) them. The apparent expression of the Quran here and in another verse (Ad-Dukhan: 37) refers specifically to one of the kings of Yemen, whose name is mentioned in some narrations as "As'ad Abu Karib." A group believes he was a man of faith who invited the people to follow the call of the Prophets, though they opposed him [2]. Then, referring to all these eight nations, He says: "Every one of them denied the messengers, so My threat [of punishment] was fulfilled" (كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ الرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ).

The fact that He says they denied "the messengers" (plural), while each of them only denied their own specific prophet, is because the collective action that stemmed from all of them was, in effect, the denial of all Prophets, even though each group denied one Prophet.

Alternatively, it is because denying one of the Prophets is considered a denial of the rest, since the content of the call of all of them is one and the same.

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 11, page 120, and Volume 15, page 330, under the commentary of Al-Hijr: 78 and Ash-Shu'ara: 176.

[2]: Read more details about the people of Tubba' in Volume 21 (under the commentary of Surah Ad-Dukhan, verse 37).

In any case, these nations denied both their Prophets and the principles of Monotheism and Resurrection, and eventually met a painful fate. Some were caught in a flood, some in a deluge, others by a thunderbolt, and some by an earthquake or other means, and they finally tasted the bitter fruit of denial.

Be certain that if this group of disbelievers standing against you continues in this state, they too shall have no better fate than those before them.

Then, He proceeds to mention another proof for the possibility of the Resurrection, saying: "Were We then worn out or fatigued by the first creation" such that We would be unable to bring about the second creation and the Resurrection?! (أَ فَعَيِينا بِالْخَلْقِ الْأَوَّلِ) [1]. Then He adds: They have no doubt about the first creation, for they recognize God as the Creator of human beings, "But they are in doubt about a new creation [the Resurrection] despite these clear proofs" (بَلْ هُمْ فِي لَبْسٍ مِنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ).

In reality, due to their whims, prejudice, and stubbornness, they are caught in a contradiction. On one hand, they consider God to be the Creator of humans in the beginning, having created them all from dust; yet on the other hand, when they reach the issue of the re-creation of humans from dust, they consider it a strange and unbelievable matter, while both are similar to each other, and "the ruling for similar things regarding what is possible and what is impossible is the same."

Thus, in these verses and the preceding ones, He argues for the issue of the Resurrection through four different paths: through the knowledge of Allah, through His power, then through the repetition of the scenes of Resurrection in the world of plants, and finally through drawing attention to the first creation.

[1]: The above sentence has an omitted part, and its implied meaning is: "Were We then fatigued by the first creation so that We would be unable to perform the second?"

Whenever we refer to other verses of the Quran regarding the Resurrection, we see these same proofs, along with others, appearing separately in various verses. The Quran, with its powerful logic and simple, decisive, and attractive expressions, has proven the issue of the physical Resurrection against the deniers in the best possible way. Had they emptied themselves of prejudices, stubbornness, and blind imitation, they would have very quickly submitted to this reality and known that the Resurrection is not a complicated matter.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ وَنَعْلَمُ مَا تُوَسْوِسُ بِهِۦ نَفْسُهُۥ ۖ وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ ٱلْوَرِيدِ (١٦)
Certainly We have created man and We know to what his soul tempts him, and We are nearer to him than his jugular vein. (16)
إِذْ يَتَلَقَّى ٱلْمُتَلَقِّيَانِ عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ ٱلشِّمَالِ قَعِيدٌ (١٧)
When the twin recorders record [his deeds], seated on the right hand and on the left: (17)
مَّا يَلْفِظُ مِن قَوْلٍ إِلَّا لَدَيْهِ رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ (١٨)
he says no word but that there is a ready observer beside him. (18)

Commentary

They Record Even Your Slightest Words!

In these verses, another aspect of the issues related to the Resurrection is presented, which is the matter of recording and documenting the deeds of human beings for the Day of Reckoning.

First, speaking of the infinite knowledge of Allah and His scientific encompassment of human beings, He says:

"We have indeed created man, and We know what his soul whispers to him" (وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ وَنَعْلَمُ مَا تُوَسْوِسُ بِهِ نَفْسُهُ).

"Tuwaswisu" is derived from the root "waswasah." According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it refers to undesirable thoughts that pass through a person's heart. Its origin is from the word "waswas," which means the sound of ornaments and also a hidden message or sound.

The point here is that when Allah is aware of the inner promptings of their hearts and the fleeting whispers that pass through their thoughts, He is certainly aware of all their beliefs, actions, and speech, and He keeps a record of everything for the Day of Account.

The phrase "We have indeed created man" (وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ) may point to the fact that it is impossible for the Creator of man to remain unaware of the details of his existence—especially a creation that is continuous and ongoing. This is because, moment by moment, the grace of existence reaches created beings from Allah; if our connection with Him were severed for a single instant, we would all perish. Just as sunlight is separated moment by moment from its source of grace, the sun, and spreads through space (in fact, as we shall explain, our connection with His Holy Essence is even higher than this).

Yes, He is the Creator, and His creation is continuous and constant, and we are dependent on His existence in all states. Given this, how could it be possible for Him to be unaware of our outward or inward selves?! At the end of the verse, to further clarify the matter, He adds: "and We are closer to him than his jugular vein" (وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ).

What a fascinating and soul-stirring expression! Our physical life depends on a vein that regularly brings blood into the heart from one side and sends it out to all organs from the other. If a pause occurs in its function for even a moment, death immediately overtakes man.

Allah is closer to us than even our heart's vein.

This is the same truth mentioned elsewhere: "And know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart, and that unto Him you shall be gathered" (Al-Anfal: 24) (وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَأَنَّهُ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ).

Of course, all of these are metaphors, and the proximity of Allah is even loftier and higher than this, though no more eloquent example can be found among perceptible things.

With this scientific encompassment of Allah, and our being within the grip of His power, our duty is clear: neither our actions nor our words are hidden from Him, nor are our thoughts, intentions, or even the whispers that pass through our hearts.

Attention to this reality awakens a person and acquaints him with his heavy responsibility and his precise record in the court of Divine Justice. It transforms an unaware and indifferent person into a vigilant, well-behaved, committed, and pious being.

It is narrated in a Hadith that one day Abu Hanifa said to Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "I saw your son Musa praying while people were passing in front of him, and he did not forbid them, even though this is not desirable!" Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said: "Call my son Musa." They called him, and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام repeated Abu Hanifa's words to his son Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام. In response, he said: "O father!

'Indeed, He for whom I was praying was closer to me than them, as Allah, the Almighty and Sublime, says: We are closer to him than his jugular vein!'"

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام embraced him and said:

"May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O you in whose heart the Divine secrets are entrusted!" [^1]

Commentators and linguists have various interpretations regarding the meaning of "Warid." Some believe that the Warid is the vein connected to the heart or the liver, while others consider it to refer to all the veins that pass through the human body. Others have interpreted it as the neck vein, sometimes calling it "Waridan," meaning the two neck veins.

However, the first meaning seems more appropriate, especially considering verse 24 of Surah Al-Anfal, which we previously mentioned.

Additionally, this word (Warid) is originally derived from the root "Wurud," meaning to go toward water. Since blood enters the heart through this vein, or enters other organs, it is called "Warid."

However, it should be noted that the current common terminology regarding "vein" (warid) and "artery" (sharyan)—veins that carry blood from all organs toward the heart, and arteries that carry blood from the heart to the organs—is a specific biological term that is not necessarily tied to the literal linguistic meaning of this word.

In the next verse, He adds: "Remember when the two receivers [angels] receive, seated on the right and on the left" (إِذْ يَتَلَقَّى الْمُتَلَقِّيانِ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ الشِّمالِ قَعِيدٌ) ^2.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 108. ^2: "Idh" (when) in the phrase "إِذْ يَتَلَقَّى الْمُتَلَقِّيانِ" is an adverbial particle related to a hidden verb, estimated as: "Remember when the two receivers receive." This meaning has been accepted by a group of commentators, while another group considers it related to "Aqrab" (closer) mentioned in the previous verse. However, the first interpretation seems more correct, as the sentence "We are closer to him than his jugular vein" and the sentence "when the two receivers receive" each maintain their independence without one restricting the other. Furthermore, the connection between the beginning and end in the second interpretation does not seem very clear.

This means that in addition to Allah's scientific encompassment of man's outward and inward self, two angels are also commissioned to guard and record the account of his deeds. They watch over him from the right and the left, staying with him constantly and never separating for a moment, so that this may serve as a further completion of the proof (itmam al-hujjah) and an emphasis on the matter of preserving the record of deeds.

"Talaqqi" means receiving, taking, and recording, and the "Mutalaqqiyan" are the two angels commissioned to record human deeds.

"Qa'id" is derived from "Qu'ud," meaning sitting [^1]. Here, it means a companion and observer. In other words, the concept of the verse is not that these two angels are literally sitting on the right and left of the person—since a person is sometimes sitting and sometimes walking—rather, this expression is a metaphor for the fact that these two are always with the person, lying in wait for his actions.

The possibility has also been suggested that they are always seated on the right and left shoulders, or the right and left canine teeth of the person, recording his deeds. Some less-known narrations also hint at this meaning (Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 59, p. 186, Hadith 32).

It is noteworthy that Islamic narrations state that the angel on the right

[^1]: "Qa'id" is singular even though "Mutalaqqiyan" is dual. This is because the verse has an omitted part, estimated as: "when the two receivers receive, one seated on the right and one seated on the left," where one was omitted due to the presence of the other.

is the writer of good deeds, and the angel on the left is the writer of sins. The first angel is the commander of the second. When a person performs a good deed, the angel on the right writes it ten times over. When an evil deed is committed and the angel on the left wants to write it, the first angel says: "Do not hurry." Thus, he delays it for seven hours; if the person regrets and repents, he writes nothing, and if he does not repent, he writes only one sin for him. [^1]

It also appears from narrations that after a believer dies, they say: "O Lord! You have taken the soul of Your servant; where is our mission now?"

He says: "My heavens are full of My angels who constantly worship Me, and the earth is full of obedient creation. Go to the grave of My servant and perform Tasbih, Takbir, and Tahlil there, and record it in the book of My servant's good deeds until the Day of Resurrection!" ^2

In another narration, it is stated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Whenever a Muslim falls ill, Allah says to the angels guarding his deeds: 'As long as he is ill, write for him the deeds he used to perform while in health.'" Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله added:

"Whoever falls ill or travels, Allah the Exalted writes for him what he used to do while healthy and at home." [^3]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 144.

[^3]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 26, p. 165 (under the verses in question). The same theme is narrated in the book Al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام (Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 59, p. 187, Hadiths 34 and 35).

All of these are meaningful indications of the expansion of Divine Grace.

The last verse under discussion again emphasizes the matter of the angels recording deeds, saying: "Man does not utter a single word but that there is with him an observer, ready [to record]" (مَا يَلْفِظُ مِنْ قَوْلٍ إِلَّا لَدَيْهِ رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ) [^1]. In the previous verse, the talk was about recording all of man's actions, but in this verse, it focuses specifically on his words and speech. This is due to the extraordinary importance and the influential role that speech plays in human life, to the extent that sometimes a single sentence changes the social path toward good or evil.

It is also because many people do not consider their words to be part of their deeds and feel free in their speech, whereas man's most effective and dangerous actions are his very words.

Therefore, mentioning this verse after the previous one is a case of mentioning the specific after the general.

"Raqib" means an observer, and "Atid" means one who is prepared to perform a task. Thus, a horse ready to run is called "Faras Atid," and someone who stores and preserves something is also called "Atid" (from the root "Atad," meaning to store).

Most commentators believe that "Raqib" and "Atid" are the same two angels mentioned in the previous verse as "Mutalaqqiyan." The angel on the right

[^1]: The pronoun in "ladayhi" refers to "qawl" (word). There is also a possibility it refers to the speaker, but the first possibility is more appropriate.

is named "Raqib" and the angel on the left is named "Atid." Although the verse under discussion is not explicit on this, considering the totality of the verses, such an interpretation does not seem far-fetched.

As for what words these two angels write, there is discussion among commentators:

A group believes they write everything, even the moan a person in pain lets out! While others believe they only write words of good and evil, obligatory and recommended, or forbidden and disliked, and have nothing to do with permissible (mubah) matters.

However, the generality of the verse's expression indicates that all of man's words and speech are recorded.

Interestingly, it is narrated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that:

"When two believers sit together and engage in private conversation, the guardians [of deeds] say to one another: 'Let us withdraw, for perhaps they have a secret which Allah has concealed for them!'"

The narrator asked: "Does Allah, the Almighty and Sublime, not say: 'Man does not utter a single word but that there is with him an observer, ready'?"

The Imam replied:

"If the guardians do not hear, then the Knower of Secrets hears and sees!" [^1]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 110.

From this narration, it is understood that out of honor and respect for the believer, Allah keeps some of his private words hidden from them, but He Himself is the Guardian of all these secrets.

It is also understood from some narrations that the angels of the night are different from the angels of the day, as we mentioned in the commentary of verse 78 of Surah Al-Isra. [^1]

Key Points The Friend is Closer to Me Than Myself!

Some philosophers say: "Just as extreme distance causes hiddenness, so does extreme proximity. For example, if the sun were very far from us, it would not be visible, and if we were very close to it, its light would be so dazzling that we would again lack the power to see it."

In truth, the Pure Essence of Allah is like this: "O He who is hidden due to the intensity of His light!" In the verses under discussion, Allah's extraordinary proximity to His servants is expressed through a fascinating metaphor: He is closer to us than our jugular vein.

This proximity stems from the intensity of our dependence on Him.

Even metaphors such as "the world is all body and He is the soul of the world," or "the world is like a ray and He is the solar disk," cannot fully express this close relationship. The best expression is that which the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, stated (in the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha):

"He is with everything, but not by way of association; and He is different from everything, but not by way of separation!"

A group of philosophers has another metaphor to express this extraordinary proximity: they have likened the Essence of Allah to a "noun" (ismi) meaning and created beings to a "particle" (harfi) meaning.

To explain: when we say "Turn toward the Kaaba," the word "toward" by itself has no distinct meaning. Until the word "Kaaba" is added to it, it remains mute, vague, and incomprehensible. Therefore, a particle meaning cannot have a concept on its own except as a subordinate to a "noun" meaning.

The existence of all beings in the world is likewise; without dependence on and connection to His Essence, they have no concept, existence, or permanence at all. This shows the ultimate proximity of Allah to His servants, and the proximity of the servants to Him, even though the unaware are heedless of this meaning.

The Friend is closer to me than myself, And stranger still is that I am far from Him! What can I do? To whom can it be said that the Friend Is by my side, yet I am estranged!

[^1]: Refer to Volume 12, page 223 (under verse 78 of Surah Al-Isra).

Ayahs

وَجَآءَتْ سَكْرَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنتَ مِنْهُ تَحِيدُ (١٩)
The throes of death bring the truth: ‘This is what you used to shun!’ (19)
وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْوَعِيدِ (٢٠)
And the Trumpet will be blown: ‘This is the promised day.’ (20)
وَجَآءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّعَهَا سَآئِقٌ وَشَهِيدٌ (٢١)
Every soul will come accompanied by [two angels], a driver, and a witness: (21)
لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِى غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَآءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ (٢٢)
[he will be told] ‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so today your eyesight is acute.’ (22)

Commentary

Resurrection and Sharp-Sighted Eyes!

In these verses, other scenes related to the "Resurrection" are reflected: The scene of "Death," the scene of the "Blowing of the Trumpet," and the scene of "Presence in the Gathering."

First, He says: "And the stupor of death will come in truth" (وَ جاءَتْ سَكْرَةُ الْمَوْتِ بِالْحَقِ).

"The stupor of death" (Sakrat al-Mawt) is a state similar to "intoxication" that overcomes a person due to the onset of death's precursors, manifesting as an extraordinary agitation and upheaval. At times, it overwhelms his intellect and plunges him into intense anxiety and restlessness.

How could it be otherwise, when death is a major transitional stage in which a person must, at that moment, sever all his ties with a world he has been accustomed to for many years, and step into a realm that is completely new and mysterious to him? This is especially true since, at the moment of death, man gains a new perception and vision; he sees the instability of this world with his own eyes and observes the events after death to some extent. It is here that a great terror envelops his entire being, and a state similar to intoxication overcomes him, though he is not "drunk." [^1] Even the Prophets and men of God, who enjoy complete tranquility at the moment of death, are not exempt from the difficulties and hardships of this transitional moment. As recorded in the accounts of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, in the final moments of his blessed life, he would dip his hand into a container of water, wipe it over his face, say "La ilaha illa Allah," and remark:

"Indeed, death has its stupors." [^2] Ali عليه السلام provides a vivid and eloquent depiction of the moment of death and its stupors, saying:

"The stupor of death and the grief of losing [what they had] descend upon them. Consequently, their limbs become feeble and their complexions change. Then death increases its penetration within them, until a barrier is placed between one of them and his speech, while he is among his family, looking with his eyes and hearing with his ears, with his intellect sound and his mind intact. He reflects on what he exhausted his life in and how he spent his time. He remembers the wealth he accumulated, closing his eyes to its sources, taking it from clear and doubtful means alike. The consequences of gathering it now cling to him, while he is on the verge of parting from it. It remains for those after him to enjoy and benefit from, [while the accountability remains his]!"

[^1]: "Sukr" (rhyming with makr) originally means blocking a watercourse, and "Sakar" (rhyming with fikr) refers to the blocked place. Since intoxication creates a barrier between a person and his intellect, it is called "Sukr" (rhyming with shukr). [^2]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 118.

"The stupors of death, coupled with the regret of losing what they possessed, assault them. Their limbs grow weak, and the color fades from their faces. Gradually, death penetrates them, separating them from their tongues, while the person is still among his family, seeing with his eyes and hearing with his ears, his intellect and consciousness still intact, yet he cannot speak! He ponders: in what path did he vanish his life? And in what way did he spend his days?! He remembers the riches for which he closed his eyes [to ethics] to obtain, gathering from the lawful, the forbidden, and the doubtful, and now he carries the burdens and responsibilities of its collection on his shoulders, while the time of separation from them has arrived. It falls into the hands of those left behind; they flourish and benefit from it, but the responsibility and accounting for it rest upon him!" [^1]

In another place, this great teacher of humanity warns, saying:

"If you could see what those of you who have died have seen, you would be distressed and terrified; you would listen to the words of truth and obey. But what they have seen is veiled from you, and soon the veils will be cast aside!" [^2]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 109. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 20.

"But what they have seen is hidden from you, and soon the curtains will be pulled back, and you too shall witness it (but alas...)."

Then the Quran continues: "It will be said to the one in the stupor of death: This is what you used to avoid and flee from!" (ذلِكَ ما كُنْتَ مِنْهُ تَحِيدُ). [^1] Yes, death is a reality that most individuals flee from, either because they consider it "annihilation" rather than a gateway to the world of "permanence," or because of the intense attachments and bonds they have with the world and its material blessings which they cannot bear to leave, or because of the darkness of their record of deeds! Whatever the reason, they flee from it. But what use is it, for this is the destiny awaiting everyone; it is the camel that kneels at everyone's door, and no one has the power to escape it. Everyone eventually falls into the jaws of death, and they are told: "This is what you were running away from." The speaker of these words may be Allah, the angels, awakened consciences, or all of them.

The Quran points out this truth in other verses as well. In Surah An-Nisa, verse 78, He says: "Wherever you may be, death will overtake you, even if you should be within lofty towers!" Sometimes a prideful person, due to selfishness and love for the world, consigns all objective realities seen with the eyes to oblivion, to the point that he swears he has an eternal life, as the Quran says: "Had you not sworn before that for you there would be no decline?" But whether he swears or not, whether he believes it or not, death is a reality that overtakes everyone, and there is no way to escape it.

Then, addressing the issue of the Blowing of the Trumpet, He says: "And the Trumpet will be blown. That is the Day of the [fulfillment of] the Warning" (وَ نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ ذلِكَ يَوْمُ الْوَعِيدِ). The "Blowing of the Trumpet" intended here is the "Second Blowing." As we have mentioned before, the Trumpet is blown twice. The first blowing, called the blowing of "terror" or "fainting," occurs at the end of the world, and all humans die upon hearing it, and the system of the worldly realm collapses. The second blowing is the blowing of "rising," "gathering," and "presence," which takes place at the beginning of the Resurrection. With it, all humans are brought back to life, rising from their graves to be present before the Divine Justice for accounting and recompense.

"Nafkh" originally means "to blow," and "Nafkhah" means "a single blow." "Sur" means a "trumpet" or "horn" usually used to give orders to soldiers to gather, stand at attention, or rest and sleep. Its use regarding the "Trumpet of Israfil" is a type of metaphor and comparison; details of this are found in Volume 19, page 537 onwards (under verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar).

In any case, considering the end of the verse (the phrase "That is the Day of the Warning"), it becomes clear that the "Blowing of the Trumpet" here refers to the second blowing and the Resurrection.

In the following verse, He describes the state of humans upon entering the Gathering: "On that day, every soul (whether righteous or wicked) shall come, accompanied by a driver and a witness" (وَ جاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَعَها سائِقٌ وَ شَهِيدٌ).

The "driver" (Sa'iq) herds him toward the court of Divine Justice, and the "witness" (Shahid) testifies to his deeds. It is exactly like the courts of this world, where an officer accompanies the accused and a witness testifies to his actions.

Some have suggested that the "driver" is the one who "drives" the righteous toward Paradise and the wicked toward Hell. However, considering the word "witness," the first meaning—driving toward the court of Divine Justice—is more appropriate.

Regarding whether this driver and witness are from among the angels or others, various interpretations have been offered: Some have said the "driver" is the angel who records good deeds, and the "witness" is the angel who records evil deeds; thus, they are the same angels mentioned in previous verses. From one narration, it is inferred that the "driver" is the Angel of Death and the "witness" is the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, but this narration seems weak given the tone of the verses. Others have said: the "driver" is an angel who drives every human, and the "witness" is the person's own deeds. It has also been said that the "driver" is an angel, and the "witness" is the person's own limbs, or the record of deeds hung around his neck. The possibility has also been raised that the "driver" and "witness" are but one angel, and the conjunction of the two is due to the difference in these two descriptions—meaning an angel is with him who both drives him to the Divine court and is a witness to his deeds.

However, most of these interpretations go against the apparent meaning of the verse. As most commentators have understood, the apparent meaning is that two angels accompany every human: one drives him and the other witnesses his deeds. It goes without saying that the testimony of some angels does not contradict the existence of other witnesses on the scene of Resurrection, such as the Prophets, the limbs of the body, the record of deeds, and the time and place where the sin was committed. In any case, the first angel effectively prevents "escape," and the second angel prevents "denial." Thus, every human on that day is held by his own deeds, and there is no way to escape their recompense and punishment.

Here, the criminals—or all humans—are addressed: "You were heedless of this [great court], so We have stripped from you your veil, and today your sight is sharp!" (لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ). Yes, the veils of the material world—hopes and desires, love and attachment to the world, spouse, children, wealth and status, rebellious passions and jealousies, prejudice, ignorance, and stubbornness—did not allow you to look at today from that time, even though the signs of the Resurrection were clear and its proofs manifest! Today, the dust of heedlessness has settled, the veils of ignorance, prejudice, and stubbornness have been removed, the curtains of lusts and desires have been torn, and even what was hidden in the realm of the unseen has become apparent. For today is the "Day of Manifestation," the "Day of Witnessing," and the "Day when secrets are tested!" For this reason, you have gained a sharp sight and can well perceive the realities! Indeed, the face of truth is not hidden, and the beauty of the Beloved has no veil, but the dust of the path must be settled so that one can behold it.

The beauty of the Beloved has no veil or curtain, But settle the dust of the path so that you may gaze!

However, sinking into the well of nature and being trapped amidst various veils does not allow man to see realities clearly. But on that day when all these attachments and bonds are severed, man naturally gains a new perception and vision. Fundamentally, the Day of Resurrection is the day of the manifestation and disclosure of realities. Even in this world, those who can cast aside these veils from before the eye of the heart and free themselves from the clutches of the captivity of lusts gain a perception and vision that the children of the world are deprived of.

It is also necessary to note that "Hadid" originally means "iron" and also means a sharp knife or sword; subsequently, it was applied to sharp-sightedness and sharp-wittedness. Just as "cutting" is an attribute of a sword or knife, in Persian, "cutting" is also applied to an eloquent tongue and speech. From this, it becomes clear that "sight" (Basar) here does not refer to the physical eye, but rather to the eye of the intellect and the heart.

Ali عليه السلام says regarding the Divine Proofs on earth: "Knowledge has rushed upon them with the reality of insight, and they have touched the spirit of certainty. They find easy what the extravagant find difficult, and they are intimate with what the ignorant are terrified of. They accompany the world with bodies whose souls are suspended in the Highest Place. They are the vicegerents of Allah on His earth and the callers to His religion." [^1]

Key Points

1- The Reality of Death? It is often imagined that death is a non-existent matter and means annihilation, but this perception never aligns with what is found in the Holy Quran and what rational proofs lead to. From the Quranic perspective, "death" is an existential matter; it is a transition and passage from one world to another. Therefore, in many Quranic verses, "death" is expressed as "Tawaffi," which means taking back and receiving the soul from the body by the angels. The expression in the above verses, "And the stupor of death will come in truth," also points to this same meaning. [^1] In some verses, death is explicitly called a creation of God: "He who created death and life" (Al-Mulk: 2).

In Islamic narrations, various expressions have appeared regarding the reality of death. In a Hadith, we read that Imam Sajjad, Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, was asked: "What is death?" He replied: "For the believer, it is like removing dirty, lice-infested clothes, and unfastening heavy chains and shackles, and replacing them with the most magnificent clothes, the most pleasant fragrances, the smoothest mounts, and the most intimate dwellings." "And for the disbeliever, it is like stripping off magnificent clothes, moving from intimate dwellings, and replacing them with the filthiest and coarsest clothes, the most terrifying dwellings, and the greatest punishment!"

Imam Muhammad ibn Ali عليه السلام was also asked the same question. He said: "It is the sleep that comes to you every night, except that its duration is long; one does not wake from it until the Day of Resurrection!" [^1] In discussions regarding the Barzakh (the intermediate realm), we have said that the state of individuals in Barzakh varies: some, as if falling into a deep sleep, and some (like the martyrs in the way of Allah and believers with strong faith) are immersed in various blessings, while a group of tyrants and the wretched are immersed in Divine punishment.

Imam Husayn ibn Ali, the Master of Martyrs عليه السلام, also provided a beautiful expression regarding the reality of death for his companions in Karbala on the day of Ashura as the battle intensified: "Be patient, O children of noble men! Death is nothing but a bridge that carries you across from distress and hardship to vast gardens and eternal bliss. Which of you dislikes moving from a prison to a palace? But for your enemies, it is like moving from a palace to a prison and punishment. My father narrated to me from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله that the world is the prison of the believer and the paradise of the disbeliever, and death is the bridge for these to their gardens, and the bridge for those to their hellfire!" [^2]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, "Short Sayings," Saying 147. [^1]: Regarding what the "Ba" in "bil-Haqq" means, various possibilities have been given. Some have taken it as the "Ba of transition," taking "Haqq" to mean death, so the meaning becomes: "The stupors of death bring with them that which is a reality—namely death." Others have taken it to mean "accompaniment," meaning "The stupors of death arrive accompanied by the truth." [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 155 (apparently referring to the ninth Imam, Imam al-Jawad عليه السلام). [^2]: Ma'ani al-Akhbar, p. 289, Chapter on the Meaning of Death, Hadith 3.

In another Hadith, we read: Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام visited someone who was immersed in the stupors of death and was not responding to anyone. The crowd said: "O son of the Messenger of Allah! We would like you to describe the reality of death for us and tell us what state our sick one is in now." He said: "Death is a means of purification that cleanses believers from sin; it is the last discomfort of this world and the atonement for their final sins. Meanwhile, it separates disbelievers from their blessings; it is the last pleasure they receive and the final reward for any good deed they might have occasionally performed. As for this dying person, he has been completely cleansed of his sins, emerged from transgressions, and become pure, just as dirty clothes are cleaned by washing. He has now gained the worthiness to be our companion, the Ahl al-Bayt, in the eternal abode!" [^1]

2- The Stupors of Death In the above verses, there was mention of "the stupors of death" (Sakrat al-Mawt). We said "Sakrat" is the plural of "Sakrah," meaning a state similar to intoxication that occurs due to the intensity of an event, making a person severely agitated, though it is not intoxication. It is true that for believers, death is the beginning of a transition to a wider world full of Divine blessings, but nevertheless, this transitional state is not easy for any human, because the soul has been accustomed to and bonded with this body for many years. Therefore, when Imam Sadiq عليه السلام was asked why the soul feels discomfort when it leaves the body, he said: "Because the body grew upon it!" [^1] It is exactly like pulling a decayed tooth from the mouth; certainly, one feels relief afterward, but the moment of separation is painful.

In some Islamic narrations, we read: "Three days are terrifying for man: the day he is born and sees this unfamiliar world, the day he dies and observes the world after death, and the day he enters the arena of Gathering and sees judgments that were not in the worldly abode. Therefore, Allah Almighty says regarding Yahya ibn Zakariyya: 'And peace be upon him the day he was born, the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive,' and He narrates similar words from the tongue of Isa ibn Maryam عليه السلام, including these two Prophets in His grace during these three days." [^2] But it is certain that those who have specific attachments to this world find their transition from it much harder, and severing their hearts from what they love more difficult. Likewise, those who have committed more sins find the stupors of death more intense and painful for them.

3- Death is "Truth" Not only in the verses under discussion are the "stupors of death" introduced as "truth" (Haqq), but in several other verses, the fact that death is truth is emphasized. In Surah Al-Hijr, verse 99, we read: "And worship your Lord until the Certainty (death) comes to you." (A similar expression is seen in Surah Al-Muddathir, verse 47). All of this is because even if a person denies everything, he cannot deny this reality—that eventually, death knocks on all our doors and takes everyone away. Attention to the truth of death is a warning for all humans to think more and better, to become aware of the path ahead of them, and to prepare themselves for it. Interestingly, it is mentioned in a Hadith: "A man came to Umar and said: 'I love fitna (trial/strife)! I hate the truth! And I testify to something I have never seen!' Umar threw him into prison. This reached the ears of Ali عليه السلام, who said: 'O Umar! Imprisoning this man is an injustice, and you have committed a wrong.' He asked, 'Why?' He replied: 'Because he loves his wealth and children, which God has referred to as "fitna" in one of the Quranic verses: "Your wealth and your children are only a trial (fitna)." [^1] He hates death, and in the Quran, it is referred to as "truth" (Haqq): "And the stupor of death will come in truth." [^2] And he testifies to the oneness of God, whom he has never seen.' At this, Umar said: 'If it were not for Ali, Umar would have perished.'" [^3]

[^1]: Same source. [^1]: Bihar, Vol. 6, p. 158. [^2]: Same source (slightly summarized). Regarding Yahya عليه السلام, we read in Surah Maryam, verse 15: "And peace be upon him the day he was born, the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive." Regarding Christ عليه السلام, in the same Surah, verse 33, we read: "And peace be upon me the day I was born, the day I die, and the day I am raised alive." [^1]: At-Taghabun: 15. [^2]: Qaf: 19. [^3]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 118.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ قَرِينُهُۥ هَـٰذَا مَا لَدَىَّ عَتِيدٌ (٢٣)
Then his companion [angel] will say, ‘This is what is ready with me [of his record of deeds].’ (23)
أَلْقِيَا فِى جَهَنَّمَ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ (٢٤)
[The two angels accompany him will be told,] ‘Cast every obdurate ingrate into hell, (24)
مَّنَّاعٍ لِّلْخَيْرِ مُعْتَدٍ مُّرِيبٍ (٢٥)
[every] hinderer of good, transgressor, and skeptic, (25)
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَأَلْقِيَاهُ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلشَّدِيدِ (٢٦)
who had set up another god along with Allah and cast him into the severe punishment.’ (26)
۞ قَالَ قَرِينُهُۥ رَبَّنَا مَآ أَطْغَيْتُهُۥ وَلَـٰكِن كَانَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٢٧)
His companion [devil] will say, ‘Our Lord! I did not incite him to rebel [against You], but he was [himself] in extreme error.’ (27)
قَالَ لَا تَخْتَصِمُوا۟ لَدَىَّ وَقَدْ قَدَّمْتُ إِلَيْكُم بِٱلْوَعِيدِ (٢٨)
He will say, ‘Do not wrangle in My presence, for I had already warned you in advance. (28)
مَا يُبَدَّلُ ٱلْقَوْلُ لَدَىَّ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (٢٩)
The word [of judgement] is unalterable with Me, and I am not tyrannical to My servants.’ (29)
يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ ٱمْتَلَأْتِ وَتَقُولُ هَلْ مِن مَّزِيدٍ (٣٠)
The day when We shall say to hell, ‘Are you full?’ It will say, ‘Is there any more?’ (30)

Commentary

The Companions of Man from the Angels and the Satans

Once again, these verses depict another scene from the Resurrection—a soul-shaking scene where the angel who is the companion of man reveals his condemnation, and Allah’s command for his punishment is issued.

First, He says: "His companion will say: 'This is [the record of deeds] with me, ready and prepared'" and uncovers all his small and large actions throughout his entire life (وَ قالَ قَرِينُهُ هذا ما لَدَيَّ عَتِيدٌ).

Regarding who is meant by "companion" (qarin) here, commentators have much discussion, but they generally accept that it refers to the angel who was with the human in the world, commissioned to record his deeds, and who now testifies in the court of Divine Justice.

The preceding verses, which stated that everyone enters the gathering place of Resurrection with a "driver" and a "witness," also support this meaning. Furthermore, the tone of this verse and the one following it aligns with this interpretation. (Reflect on this). However, some have said that "companion" here refers to "Satan," because in many verses of the Quran, this word is applied to the Satan who is the associate of criminals. According to this interpretation, the meaning of the verse would be: "His companion Satan says: 'I have made this criminal ready for Hell, and I have exerted the utmost effort within my power in this path!'"

But this meaning is not only inconsistent with the preceding verses and the verse immediately following this one, but it also does not harmonize with Satan’s exoneration of himself from the sin of seducing humans, which appears a few verses later. According to that interpretation, Satan would be confessing to his responsibility in seducing the criminals, whereas in the upcoming verses we read: قالَ قَرِينُهُ رَبَّنا ما أَطْغَيْتُهُ وَ لكِنْ كانَ فِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ: "His companion will say: 'Our Lord! I did not make him transgress, but he himself was in far-reaching error,'" which is in complete contradiction to it.

A third interpretation has also been mentioned here, which seems the most remote of all and is supported by no evidence: that "companion" refers to human friends and associates.

Then, Allah addresses the two angels commissioned to record deeds, saying: "Cast into Hell every stubborn, arrogant disbeliever" (أَلْقِيا فِي جَهَنَّمَ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ).

"Anid" (stubborn) is derived from the root "inad," meaning arrogance, self-conceit, and refusal to submit to the Truth.

Regarding who these two addressed individuals are, various interpretations have been offered. A group has chosen the above interpretation (the two recording angels), while others consider the addressees to be the "keepers of Hell" (two of the officials of Jahannam).

Some have also said that the addressee might be only one person—the same witness who enters the gathering place with the criminal, as mentioned in previous verses—and the use of the dual form of the verb is for emphasis, as if He repeats twice: "Cast! Cast!" (Alqi! Alqi!). The use of the dual for a single addressee exists in the Arabic language, but this interpretation seems very remote, and the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all.

In the next verse, He points to several ugly and blameworthy traits of these stubborn disbelievers, saying: "He who is a hinderer of good, a transgressor, and a doubter—rather, one who casts others into doubt" (مَنَّاعٍ لِلْخَيْرِ مُعْتَدٍ مُرِيبٍ). "Mannā’" (hinderer), being an intensive form, refers to someone who frequently prevents something; therefore, "mannā’in lil-khayr" is someone who opposes every good deed in every way.

In some narrations, it is mentioned that this verse was revealed regarding "Walid ibn Mughirah," when he told his brother's children: "Whoever among you accepts Islam, I will never help him as long as I live." [1] "Mu’tad" means a transgressor, whether he violates the rights of others or exceeds the limits of Divine laws.

[1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 26, p. 168.

"Murib" is derived from "rayb," meaning a person who is in doubt—a doubt coupled with pessimism—or one who casts others into doubt through his speech and actions, causing their misguidance.

Continuing the description of the traits of this stubborn group, the next verse adds: "The one who set up another deity with Allah and took the path of polytheism and duality" (الَّذِي جَعَلَ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ). Yes, "cast such a person into the severe punishment" (فَأَلْقِياهُ فِي الْعَذابِ الشَّدِيدِ).

In these few verses, six descriptions are given for this hell-bound group. The first five are actually causes and effects of one another, and the sixth description is an explanation for the primary root of all these traits, because:

"Kaffar" means one who is very persistent in disbelief. And this leads to "inad" (stubbornness). A stubborn person also insists on preventing good deeds ("mannā’in lil-khayr"), and such a person is naturally a transgressor ("mu’tad") of the rights of others and Divine limits. Transgressing individuals insist on casting others into doubt ("murib") and stripping them of faith.

In this way, the five traits of "kaffar," "anid," "mannā’in lil-khayr," "mu’tad," and "murib" have an unbreakable bond and are prerequisites and consequences of one another. [1] In the sixth description, namely الَّذِي جَعَلَ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ, the primary root of all

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 18, p. 381.

these deviations—which is polytheism (shirk)—is mentioned, for upon careful reflection, it becomes clear that shirk is the factor behind all these misfortunes.

The next verse pulls back the curtain on another event in the fate of this stubborn disbelieving group: the dispute and argument they have with Satan on the Day of Resurrection. They cast all their sins upon the shoulders of the seductive Satans, but "his companion Satan says: 'Our Lord, I did not make him transgress, nor did I bring him to this path by force; he chose this path himself by his own will and desire, and he was in far-reaching error'" (قالَ قَرِينُهُ رَبَّنا ما أَطْغَيْتُهُ وَ لكِنْ كانَ فِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ). This expression is similar to what appears in Surah Ibrahim, verse 22, where Satan says to exonerate himself: وَ ما كانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي فَلا تَلُومُونِي وَ لُومُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ: "I had no authority over you except that I invited you and you responded to me. So do not blame me, but blame yourselves!"

Of course, Satan does not want to completely deny his role in seducing man; rather, he wants to prove that there was no compulsion involved and that man accepted his whispers by his own inclination and desire. Therefore, it does not contradict the verse: لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ: "I will surely seduce them all" (Sad: 82).

Although in these verses only Satan's defense is mentioned and no mention is made of the disbelievers' objection to Satan, by the evidence of other Quranic verses where they argue with each other on the Day of Resurrection, and by the evidence of the following verse, the speech of both parties becomes clear in summary.

For in the next verse we read: "Allah says: 'Do not dispute in My presence; I had already sent you the warning'" and informed you of this grim fate (قالَ لا تَخْتَصِمُوا لَدَيَّ وَ قَدْ قَدَّمْتُ إِلَيْكُمْ بِالْوَعِيدِ). This refers to the fact that, on one hand, I said to Satan: اذْهَبْ فَمَنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزاؤُكُمْ جَزاءً مَوْفُوراً: "Go! Whoever of them follows you, then indeed Hell will be the ample recompense for all of you" (Al-Isra: 63).

And on the other hand, I warned humans: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَ مِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ: "I will surely fill Hell with you and those who follow you, all together" (Sad: 85). These threats and warnings have appeared in other verses of the Quran as well, and all indicate that Allah has established the proof (itman al-hujjah) for both humans and Satans, warning them against seducing and being seduced.

Then, for further emphasis, He adds: "My word is unchangeable, and the word is not altered with Me, and I am never unjust to My servants" (ما يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَّ وَ ما أَنَا بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ) [1]. The "word" (qawl) here refers to the threats and warnings that Allah has mentioned in various verses, examples of which were cited above. The use of "Zallām" in the intensive form (one who is very unjust), even though Allah does not permit the slightest injustice, may indicate that Allah’s station of knowledge, power, and justice is such that if He were to do even a small injustice to someone, it would be considered great

[1]: "Ladayya" (with Me) is related to "yubaddalu" (is altered). Some have also suggested it relates to "qawl" (word), but the first meaning is more appropriate.

and significant, and would be an instance of being "Zallām." Therefore, He is far removed from any kind of injustice. Or it may refer to the multitude of individuals and instances; for if He were to do a small injustice to one servant, there are many similar individuals, and collectively it would become a great injustice.

In any case, this expression is evidence of the free will and choice of the servants. Neither is Satan forced to act satanically, nor are the disbelievers forced to take the path of disbelief, stubbornness, and the path of Satan, nor has a definitive fate outside of one's will been decreed for anyone.

Here the question arises: How does He say, "My word is unchangeable," while a group is included in His forgiveness? The answer is that forgiveness is also according to a calculated plan, and it is contingent upon the human having performed an action that, while being a crime, possesses the capacity and merit for forgiveness. This itself is one of the Divine traditions—to include those worthy of forgiveness in His mercy—and this too is unchangeable.

In the last verse under discussion, He points to a short and soul-shaking passage of the events of Resurrection, saying: "Remember the Day when We say to Hell: 'Are you filled?!' and it replies: 'Is there any more?'" (يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ وَ تَقُولُ هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ) [1]. Regarding what is meant by "هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ" (Is there any more?), two interpretations have been given:

[1]: Regarding what "Yawm" (Day) is related to here, there are three views: First, it relates to a hidden "Idhkuru" (Remember), and the addressees are all humans. Second, it relates to "yubaddalu." Third, it relates to "Zallām" in the previous verse. But the first possibility seems most appropriate.

First, the interrogation is a rhetorical denial, meaning Hell says: "More than this is not possible." In this way, it is completely harmonious with verse 13 of Surah As-Sajdah, which says: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَ النَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ: "I will surely fill Hell with jinn and men all together," and it is an emphasis on the fact that the Divine threat will be fully realized on that day and Hell will be brimming with disbelievers and criminals.

Second, the sentence means a request for more, meaning: "Are there still other individuals to be found who will come to Hell?" Fundamentally, the nature of everything is to constantly seek its own kind and it is never satiated—neither Paradise with the righteous, nor Hell with the wicked.

But the question remains that the implication of this speech is that Hell is not yet full, which is inconsistent with the verse above (Surah As-Sajdah, verse 13) which says: "I will fill Hell with jinn and men." However, it should be noted that requesting more is not evidence of not being full. For "firstly," a vessel might be full of food, yet someone might still desire for it to be heaped up and compressed. "Secondly," this request might mean a request to tighten the space for the inhabitants of Hell for a more painful punishment, or a desire to expand and then accept more people.

In any case, this verse clearly shows that the inhabitants of Hell are many, and Hell has a horrific and terrifying sight, and the Divine threat is serious. It is such that thinking about it sends a shiver through the body of every human and warns: "Lest you be one of those people!" And this very thought can control him against large and small sins.

Another question is: How can Hell, which is a being devoid of consciousness, be addressed and give an answer? There are three answers to this question:

First, this is a type of personification and an expression of the "language of state" (lisan al-hal). That is, Allah asks Hell through the language of "creation" (takwin) and it answers through the "language of state." Similar expressions are abundant in various languages.

Second, the abode of the Hereafter is the abode of real life and existence; even entities such as Paradise and Hell possess a type of life, perception, and consciousness. Paradise is intensely longing for the believers, and Hell is intensely waiting for the criminals. In a place where the limbs of a human's body speak and give testimony, it is not surprising that Paradise and Hell should be so. Rather, according to some, even in this world, all particles of this universe possess a type of perception and consciousness, and therefore they glorify and praise Allah, which is reflected in various verses of the Quran. [1]

Third, the addressees are the officials and keepers of Hell, and it is they who answer. All these interpretations are acceptable, although the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

[1]: Refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 12, p. 133 (under verse 44 of Surah Al-Isra).

Ayahs

وَأُزْلِفَتِ ٱلْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ (٣١)
And paradise will be brought near for the Godwary, it will not be distant [any more]: (31)
هَـٰذَا مَا تُوعَدُونَ لِكُلِّ أَوَّابٍ حَفِيظٍ (٣٢)
‘This is what you were promised. [It is] for every penitent and dutiful [servant] (32)
مَّنْ خَشِىَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَجَآءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُّنِيبٍ (٣٣)
who fears the All-beneficent in secret and comes with a penitent heart. (33)
ٱدْخُلُوهَا بِسَلَـٰمٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْخُلُودِ (٣٤)
Enter it in peace! This is the day of immortality.’ (34)
لَهُم مَّا يَشَآءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ (٣٥)
There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more. (35)
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُم بَطْشًا فَنَقَّبُوا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ هَلْ مِن مَّحِيصٍ (٣٦)
How many generations We have destroyed before them, who were stronger than these, insomuch that they ransacked the towns?! So, is there any escape [from Allah’s punishment]? (36)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَذِكْرَىٰ لِمَن كَانَ لَهُۥ قَلْبٌ أَوْ أَلْقَى ٱلسَّمْعَ وَهُوَ شَهِيدٌ (٣٧)
There is indeed an admonition in that for one who has a heart, or gives ear, being attentive. (37)

Commentary

O Criminals! There is No Escape

Given that the discussions in this Surah primarily revolve around the issue of the Resurrection and related matters, and considering that the previous verses spoke of how the stubborn disbelievers are cast into Hell, the severity of their punishment, and the attributes that lead them to the Fire, the verses under discussion depict another scene: the entrance of the God-fearing into Paradise with full honor. It points to the various types of heavenly blessings and the qualities that place a person among the inhabitants of Paradise, so that through comparison, the truths may become clearer.

First, He says: "On that day, Paradise shall be brought near to the God-fearing, and it shall not be far from them!" (وَ أُزْلِفَتِ الْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ).

"Uzlifat" is derived from the root "zulfa," meaning proximity and nearness.

Interestingly, it does not say the God-fearing are brought near to Paradise, but rather that Paradise is brought near to them! This is something unimaginable under the conditions of this world. However, since the principles governing the Hereafter are vastly different from the conditions of this world, it is not at all surprising that Allah, for the ultimate honor and respect of the God-fearing believers, brings Paradise to them instead of taking them to Paradise! In Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verses 90 and 91, we also read: وَ أُزْلِفَتِ الْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ وَ بُرِّزَتِ الْجَحِيمُ لِلْغاوِينَ: "And Paradise shall be brought near to the God-fearing, and Hell shall be made manifest for the erring."

This represents the ultimate grace and honor from Allah toward His believing servants, beyond which nothing can be imagined.

The expression "ghayra ba'id" (not far) serves as an emphasis. [^1] In any case, the meaning of the verse is that this event will occur on the Day of Resurrection, even though it is expressed in the past tense (uzlifat), because certain events that will happen in the future are often expressed in the past tense in many linguistic forms. However, some have interpreted it literally as the past tense, stating that the bringing near of Paradise to the God-fearing occurs in this world, as Paradise is not far from them; departing from this world is synonymous with stepping into Paradise! But considering the preceding and succeeding verses which speak of the scene of Resurrection, this meaning seems unlikely, and the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Then, in describing the attributes of the inhabitants of Paradise, He says: "This is what you were promised; it is for every one who turned back [to Allah's command] and was a guardian [of His covenants and laws]" (هذا ما تُوعَدُونَ لِكُلِّ أَوَّابٍ حَفِيظٍ).

[^1]: "Ghayra ba'id" could be an "adverb of place" (zarf) or a "circumstantial clause" (hal), or an adjective for an omitted infinitive, implied as "izlafan ghayra ba'id."

Here, two of their attributes are mentioned: "Awwab" and "Hafiz."

"Awwab" is derived from "awb," meaning to return. It may mean repenting from small and large sins, or returning to His obedience. Given that it is an "intensive form" (sighah al-mubalaghah), it shows that the inhabitants of Paradise are God-fearing people who, whenever any factor seeks to distance them from obedience to Allah, immediately take notice and return to His obedience, repenting for their shortcomings and negligences until they reach the station of the "soul at peace" (nafs al-mutma'innah).
