"Hafiz" means a protector or guardian. Does it refer to guarding the divine covenants and pledges taken from humans to obey Him and not worship Satan (Ya-Sin: 60)? Or guarding the divine laws and limits? Or keeping sins in mind and recalling them for repentance and compensation? Or all of these?

Since this decree is mentioned in an absolute sense, the latter interpretation, which is comprehensive, seems more appropriate.

Continuing these descriptions in the next verse, He points to two other attributes which are, in fact, an explanation and commentary on the previous ones. He says: "The one who feared the All-Merciful in secret and came with a repentant heart" (مَنْ خَشِيَ الرَّحْمنَ بِالْغَيْبِ وَ جاءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُنِيبٍ).

The expression "fearing Allah in secret" indicates that although they never see Allah with their eyes, they believe in Him through His signs and through reasoning and proof—a faith coupled with a full sense of responsibility.

It is also possible that it means "hidden from the eyes of people," meaning they do not commit sins not only in public but also in solitude and privacy.

This fear and awe (khashyah) cause their hearts to be "munib" (repentant), constantly turning toward Allah, inclining toward His obedience, repenting from every sin and slip, and maintaining this state until the end of their lives, entering the arena of Resurrection in this very state.

Then He adds: Those who possess these four attributes, when Paradise is brought near to them, "the angels of Allah, as a mark of respect and honor, say to them: Enter it in peace" (ادْخُلُوها بِسَلامٍ).

Peace from every kind of evil, discomfort, affliction, calamity, punishment, and torment; complete peace in terms of both body and soul.

Then, to reassure them, they add: "This is the Day of Eternity—eternity of blessings, and eternity of Paradise with all its bounties" (ذلِكَ يَوْمُ الْخُلُودِ).

Following these two favors (the glad tidings of peace and the glad tidings of eternity in Paradise), the Gracious Allah gives them two more glad tidings, making a total of four, matching the four attributes they possessed. He says: "They shall have whatever they desire therein" (لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها).

And in addition to that, "with Us is yet more"—blessings that have never even occurred to them to desire (وَ لَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ).

No expression more vivid, eloquent, and soul-stirring than this can be imagined. First, it says the inhabitants of Paradise will have whatever they wish (with the full breadth of this phrase's meaning) of all types of bounties without exception at their disposal. And beyond this are blessings and favors that have never occurred to any human mind nor crossed anyone's heart, but the All-Merciful and All-Compassionate Allah, who has encompassed the God-fearing in Paradise with His special favors, grants them those blessings as well. Thus, the heavenly blessings take on extraordinarily vast dimensions that cannot be described by any words.

Furthermore, it is understood from this expression that there is no balance between the divine reward and the deeds of the believers; rather, it is far beyond and much higher. In this stage, we encounter His grace everywhere, just as in His punishment we encounter His justice.

After concluding the discourse on Paradise and Hell, the attributes of their inhabitants, and their ranks and depths, to draw a complete conclusion from this discussion, He addresses the criminals, saying: "How many a generation have We destroyed before them who were stronger and mightier than them, who traversed the lands and dominated the cities, but were destroyed due to their disbelief, oppression, tyranny, and sin" (وَ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُمْ بَطْشاً فَنَقَّبُوا فِي الْبِلادِ).

"Is there any place of escape from death and divine punishment for such individuals?" (هَلْ مِنْ مَحِيصٍ).

"Qarn" and "iqtiran" originally mean the joining of two things or objects together. A group of people living at the same time is called a "qarn" (generation), and its plural is "qurun." Later, this term was applied to a period of time, sometimes said to be thirty years and sometimes a hundred years.

Therefore, destroying "generations" means destroying "past nations."

"Batsh" means seizing something with force and power, and sometimes it refers to war and conflict.

"Naqqabu" is from the root "naqb," meaning a hole made in a wall or skin, whereas "thaqb" refers only to a hole made in wood.

When this word is used as a verb, it means traveling, moving, and so-called "cleaving the way" and advancing. It also means conquering countries and penetrating various regions.

"Manqabah" (virtue/merit) is also from this root, referring to the deeds and attributes of prominent individuals because of the influence and impact they have on people or because it opens a path for the owner's progress.

A "naqib" (leader/overseer) is someone who investigates a group, is aware of their circumstances, and penetrates among them.

"Mahis" is from the root "hays," meaning deviation and turning away from something. Accordingly, it also means escaping from difficulties and fleeing the battlefield.

In any case, the verse warns the stubborn disbelievers contemporary with the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to look into the history of those who came before them and observe their traces on the pages of history and the face of the earth. Let them see what Allah did with the rebellious nations before them—nations that were more populous and powerful than them—and then let them reflect on their own future.

This theme appears many times in the Holy Quran, including in Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 8: فَأَهْلَكْنا أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ بَطْشاً: "So We destroyed those who were stronger than them in power."

Some consider the verse under discussion to refer to the "People of Thamud" who lived in the mountainous land of "Al-Hijr" in northern Hijaz, who carved out the mountains and built magnificent houses and palaces therein. However, it appears that the verse has a broad meaning and includes them and others.

The phrase "هَلْ مِنْ مَحِيصٍ" (Is there any place of escape?) might be from the tongues of the past nations who asked this of one another when caught in the grip of punishment, or it might be from the Lord regarding the stubborn disbelievers contemporary with the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family)—meaning, can these people escape the painful fate that the rebellious nations of the past faced?

In the final verse under discussion, for further emphasis, He adds: "Indeed, in the stories of the predecessors, there is a reminder and an admonition for him who has a heart, or gives ear while he is present!" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَذِكْرى‌ لِمَنْ كانَ لَهُ قَلْبٌ أَوْ أَلْقَى السَّمْعَ وَ هُوَ شَهِيدٌ).

The meaning of "heart" (qalb) here and in other verses of the Quran that discuss the understanding of matters is "intellect" ('aql), consciousness, and perception. In dictionaries, one of the meanings of "qalb" is stated to be "intellect." Raghib in "Al-Mufradat" interpreted "qalb" in the verse under discussion as knowledge and understanding. In "Lisan al-Arab," we also read that "qalb" is sometimes used to mean intellect. [^1] In a tradition from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the interpretation of this verse, it is stated: "The meaning of heart is intellect." [^2] The root of this word (qalb) originally means change and transformation. Since human thought and intellect are constantly in a state of flux, it is called "qalb." For this reason, Quranic verses emphasize the issue of "sakinah" (tranquility) and "itmi'nan" (assurance) of the heart: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ: "He it is who sent down tranquility into the hearts of the believers" (Al-Fath: 4); أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ: "Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah do hearts find rest" (Ar-Ra'd: 28). Yes, only the remembrance of Allah brings peace to this restless entity.

[^1]: "Lisan al-Arab," root "q-l-b." [^2]: "Usul al-Kafi," Vol. 1, "The Book of Intellect and Ignorance," Hadith 11.

"Alqa al-sam'" (cast the ear) is a metaphor for listening and the utmost precision in hearing, similar to the expression we have in Persian: "Our ear is with you," meaning we are listening carefully to your words.

"Shahid" here means one who has "presence of heart," whose mind is present in the gathering, and who follows the matters with precision.

Thus, the overall meaning of the verse is that two groups can take heed from these admonitions: first, those who possess intelligence, intellect, and wit, and can independently analyze matters; and second, those who are not at that level but can be good "listeners" to the scholars, listening to their words with presence of heart and learning the truths through their guidance and direction.

A similar statement appears in Surah Al-Mulk, verse 10, quoting the inhabitants of Hell: لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ: "Had we but listened or used our intelligence, we would not have been among the companions of the Blaze." This is because the signs of the path of truth are manifest; those who are themselves people of research perceive them well, and those who are not can find their way through the guidance of just and compassionate scholars. Therefore, a person must either have sufficient knowledge and intellect or a listening ear. [^1]

[^1]: Note that in both verses, these two matters are joined by the word "or" (aw), which indicates that at least one of these two is necessary.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَمَا مَسَّنَا مِن لُّغُوبٍ (٣٨)
Certainly We created the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them, in six days, and any fatigue did not touch Us. (38)
فَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ ٱلْغُرُوبِ (٣٩)
So be patient at what they say, and celebrate the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before the sunset, (39)
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَأَدْبَـٰرَ ٱلسُّجُودِ (٤٠)
and glorify Him through part of the night and after the prostrations. (40)

Commentary

The Creator of the Heavens and the Earth is Capable of Reviving the Dead

Following the previous verses and the various proofs regarding the Resurrection mentioned therein, these verses point to another proof of the possibility of the Resurrection. Subsequently, they command the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to practice patience, endurance, and to glorify and praise his Lord, so that he may endure and neutralize the obstructions of the opponents through this means.

First, He says: "And indeed We created the heavens and the earth and all that is between them in six days (six periods), and no fatigue or weakness touched Us in their creation!" (وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَمَا مَسَّنَا مِنْ لُغُوبٍ).

"Lughub" (لغوب) means fatigue and exhaustion.

It is obvious that one whose power is limited, if they wish to perform a task beyond their capacity, will become tired, helpless, and exhausted. However, regarding a Being whose power is unlimited and whose ability is infinite, these concepts have no meaning.

Therefore, He who is capable of creating these magnificent heavens and earth, and all these spheres, stars, and galaxies without any fatigue or hardship, is certainly capable of bringing man back to life after death and clothing his frame in the garment of life once more.

Some have narrated a "Reason for Revelation" (Shan al-Nuzul) for this verse: the Jews believed that Allah created the heavens and the earth in six days (six days of the week!), then rested on Saturday and placed one leg over the other! For this reason, they consider sitting in such a manner undesirable and exclusive to God! The above verse was revealed [^1] and put an end to such ridiculous superstitions.

However, this reason for revelation does not prevent the verse from pursuing the issue of the possibility of the Resurrection, while simultaneously being a proof of the Monotheism (Tawhid), knowledge, and power of the Lord who created the heavens and the earth with all these wonders, marvels, millions upon millions of living beings, and extraordinary secrets and specific systems. Contemplating even a corner of it can guide us toward the powerful Creator whose hand of power set this great wheel in motion and scattered the light of life everywhere.

The subject of the creation of the heavens and the earth in "six days" has appeared repeatedly in the verses of the Quran [^1]. The word "Yawm" (day)—as we have stated previously—in the Arabic language, and its equivalent "Ruz" in Persian, or in other languages, is used in many instances to mean a "period" or "era," not necessarily twenty-four hours or twelve hours. For example, we say: "One day the people lived under the shadow of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and another day the tyrants of the Umayyads and Abbasids dominated the people."

It is clear that "day" in these expressions means a "period," whether it be one year, a hundred years, or thousands or millions of years. For instance, we say one day the Earth was a mass of fire, and another day it cooled down and became ready for life; all these expressions refer to periods.

Therefore, it is understood from the above verse that Allah created the heavens, the earth, and all their inhabitants in six periods.

(We have explained this matter in detail in Volume 6, pages 200 to 204, under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf).

Thus, no room remains for the question of how there could be days and nights before the creation of the sun and the earth for Allah to have created the world in six days.

After mentioning various proofs of the Resurrection and depicting different scenes of the Day of Judgment, since a group remains who do not submit to the truth and persist in their stubbornness and adherence to falsehood, the verse addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "So be patient over what they say" (فَاصْبِرْ عَلَى مَا يَقُولُونَ).

For it is only through the power of patience and steadfastness that one can overcome these difficulties, break the conspiracies of the enemy, and endure their unjust accusations in the path of truth.

And since patience and steadfastness require a support system, and the best support is the remembrance of Allah and connection with the Source of Knowledge and Power of the universe, He adds following this command: "And glorify the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting" (وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ الشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ الْغُرُوبِ).

Likewise, "And in part of the night glorify Him, and after the prostrations" (وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَأَدْبَارَ السُّجُودِ).

This constant remembrance and continuous glorification fall like life-giving drops of rain upon the land of your heart and soul, saturating it, constantly granting you vitality and life, and inviting you to steadfastness against stubborn opponents.

Regarding what is meant by the glorification of Allah at these four times (before sunrise, before sunset, at night, and after prostrations), there is much discussion among commentators.

Some believe that these expressions refer to the five daily prayers and some of the highly meritorious supererogatory (Nafilah) prayers, such that "before the rising of the sun" refers to the Fajr (Dawn) prayer, as its end time is sunrise.

And "before the setting of the sun" refers to the Zuhr and Asr prayers, as the end time for both is sunset.

"And in part of the night" describes the Maghrib and Isha prayers, and "after the prostrations" refers to the Maghrib Nafilah prayers performed after Maghrib.

Ibn Abbas accepted this interpretation, with the proviso that he considered "after the prostrations" to refer to all Nafilah prayers performed after the obligatory ones. However, since among the daily Nafilah prayers, in our view, only the Maghrib and Isha Nafilahs are performed after these prayers, this generalization is not accurate.

Others have considered "before the rising of the sun" to refer to the Fajr prayer, "before the setting" to the Asr prayer, and "and in part of the night glorify Him" to the Maghrib and Isha prayers; thus, without any clear reason, the Zuhr prayer is not mentioned, which is a sign of the weakness of this interpretation.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about the verse "And glorify the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting," he said:

"You say ten times when you reach the morning and when you reach the evening: 'There is no god but Allah, alone without partner; to Him belongs the kingdom and to Him belongs the praise; He gives life and He gives death, and He is powerful over all things' (لا اله الا اللَّه وحده لا شريك له، له الملك، و له الحمد، يحيى و يميت، و هو على كل شى‌ء قدير)." [^1]

This interpretation does not contradict the first one, and it is possible that both are encompassed in the meaning of the verse.

It is noteworthy that a similar meaning with a slight difference appears in verse 130 of Surah Ta-Ha, where it says: "And glorify the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting, and during the hours of the night glorify Him, and at the ends of the day, that you may be satisfied" (وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ الشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ غُرُوبِهَا وَمِنْ آنَاءِ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْ وَأَطْرَافَ النَّهارِ لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضَى).

The phrase "that you may be satisfied" (لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضَى) shows that these acts of worship and glorifications play an important role in the peace of mind and contentment of man, granting him strength and ability in the face of difficult events.

It is also noteworthy that in verse 49 of Surah al-Tur, it is stated: "And in part of the night glorify Him and at the retreat of the stars" (وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَإِدْبَارَ النُّجُومِ). In a Hadith, it is narrated that Imam Ali عليه السلام said: "'After the prostrations' (أَدْبَارَ السُّجُودِ) refers to the two units of Nafilah prayer performed after Maghrib (note that the Maghrib Nafilah is four units, but here only two are mentioned), and 'at the retreat of the stars' (إِدْبَارَ النُّجُومِ) refers to the two units of Fajr Nafilah performed before the Fajr prayer at the time the stars disappear." [^2] In another narration, it is stated that "after the prostrations" refers to the Witr prayer performed at the end of the night. ^3 In any case, the first interpretation seems most appropriate, although the breadth of the concept of glorification includes many other interpretations mentioned in the narrations.

Key Point: Patience is the Secret to Every Victory

This is not the first time the Holy Quran emphasizes patience and endurance in the face of difficulties and stubborn, antagonistic individuals. Repeatedly, the Holy Quran reminds both the great Prophet of Islam and the general body of believers of this important matter. Numerous experiences have also shown that victory belongs to those who possess a greater share of patience and steadfastness.

In a Hadith, we read that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said to one of his friends (who perhaps was becoming restless in the difficult conditions of that time):

"You must observe patience in all your affairs."

He then added that Allah commissioned Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and commanded him to "patience" and leniency. He remained patient even when they attributed very improper things to him, to the point that his chest became constricted. Allah then revealed this verse to him: "And We already know that your breast is constrained by what they say. So glorify the praise of your Lord and be among those who prostrate" (وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرُكَ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَكُنْ مِنَ السَّاجِدِينَ).

Again, they denied him and threw arrows of accusation at him from every side, and he became sorrowful and grieved because of this. Again, Allah revealed this verse to comfort him: "We know that what they say grieves you, but they do not deny you; rather, the wrongdoers deny the signs of Allah. And messengers before you were certainly denied, but they were patient over being denied and harmed until Our help came to them" (قَدْ نَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُ لَيَحْزُنُكَ الَّذِي يَقُولُونَ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ وَلَكِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ وَلَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فَصَبَرُوا عَلَى مَا كُذِّبُوا وَأُوذُوا حَتَّى أَتَاهُمْ نَصْرُنَا).

Then the Imam عليه السلام added: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله forced himself to be patient, but at this point, they exceeded the limit and mentioned the name of Allah, denying His holy presence. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "I was patient regarding insults to myself, my family, and my reputation, but I have no patience regarding the vilification of my Lord." Here, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed this verse (the verse under discussion): "And indeed We created the heavens and the earth and all that is between them..." We created the heavens and the earth and what is between them in six periods (and We did not rush or hurry in the creation of the world) and no fatigue or hardship touched Us. Therefore, you too do not hurry and be patient against their words. It was here that the Prophet adopted patience and endurance in all circumstances (until he triumphed over his enemies). [^1]

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," under the verses discussed. Therefore, "Adbar al-Sujud" means "after the prostrations," and "Idbar al-Nujum" means "at the time the stars turn back." [^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," under the verses discussed.

[^1]: "Usul al-Kafi" (according to Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 117).

Ayahs

وَٱسْتَمِعْ يَوْمَ يُنَادِ ٱلْمُنَادِ مِن مَّكَانٍ قَرِيبٍ (٤١)
And be on the alert for the day when the caller calls from a close quarter, (41)
يَوْمَ يَسْمَعُونَ ٱلصَّيْحَةَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْخُرُوجِ (٤٢)
the day when they hear the Cry in all truth. That is the day of rising [from the dead]. (42)
إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْىِۦ وَنُمِيتُ وَإِلَيْنَا ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٤٣)
Indeed it is We who give life and bring death, and toward Us is the [final] destination. (43)
يَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ ٱلْأَرْضُ عَنْهُمْ سِرَاعًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ حَشْرٌ عَلَيْنَا يَسِيرٌ (٤٤)
The day the earth is split open for [disentombing] them, [they will come out] hastening. That mustering is easy for Us [to carry out]. (44)
نَّحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِجَبَّارٍ ۖ فَذَكِّرْ بِٱلْقُرْءَانِ مَن يَخَافُ وَعِيدِ (٤٥)
We know well what they say, and you are not there to compel them. So admonish by the Quran those who fear My threat. (45)

Commentary

Everyone is Resurrected by the Shout of the Resurrection

These verses, which constitute the final verses of Surah Qaf, emphasize the issue of the Hereafter and the Resurrection, just like the other verses of this Surah. They present another aspect of it: the "Blowing of the Trumpet" and the "Emergence of the dead from the graves."

He says: "And listen for the Day when the Caller will call out from a place that is near" (وَ اسْتَمِعْ يَوْمَ يُنادِ الْمُنادِ مِنْ مَكانٍ قَرِيبٍ).

"The Day when they will hear the Shout in truth; that is the Day of Emergence!" (يَوْمَ يَسْمَعُونَ الصَّيْحَةَ بِالْحَقِّ ذلِكَ يَوْمُ الْخُرُوجِ). Although the addressee in "Listen" (اسْتَمِعْ) is the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the intended meaning certainly encompasses all human beings.

The meaning of "listening" is either to wait expectantly—since those awaiting an event that begins with a terrifying sound are constantly listening and waiting—or it means listening to this Divine Word, in which case the meaning becomes: "Hear this word which your Lord says regarding the Shout of the Resurrection." [^1] But who is this Caller? It is possible that it is the Pure Essence of Allah Himself who gives this call, but more likely it is "Israfil" who blows into the "Trumpet" (Sur), and who is referred to in the verses of the Quran not by name but by other descriptions.

The expression "from a place that is near" (مَكانٍ قَرِيبٍ) indicates that this sound will be so distributed in space that it is as if it is right next to everyone's ear, and everyone hears it equally from nearby. Today, through various means, we can all hear the words of a speaker talking in a distant part of the world from up close, as if he is sitting next to us and speaking to us. However, on that Day, without the need for such means, everyone will hear the voice of the Caller of Truth, who cries out the Resurrection, in their immediate vicinity. [^2] In any case, this Shout is not the first Shout which is for the end of the world, but rather the second Shout—the Shout of rising and gathering. In fact, the second verse is an explanation and commentary for the first verse, stating: The day they hear the Shout in truth is the day of emerging from the graves and from within the dust of the earth.

To clarify who the Judge is in this great court, He adds: "Indeed, it is We who give life and cause death, and to Us is the final return" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْيِي وَ نُمِيتُ وَ إِلَيْنَا الْمَصِيرُ).

[^1]: According to the first interpretation, "Day" (يوم) is the object of "Listen" (استمع). According to the second interpretation, the object of "Listen" is omitted and implied as: "Listen to the discourse of your Lord." In this case, the accusative case of "Day" is due to a verb derived from "the Day of Emergence," meaning "they shall emerge on the Day the Caller calls."

[^2]: A group of commentators have suggested the possibility that the "near place" is the "Rock of Jerusalem" (the specific stone in Jerusalem from which the Prophet's Ascension to the heavens began), and the Caller stands beside it and cries out: "O decayed bones! O severed joints! O shredded flesh! Rise for the Final Judgment and the reward Allah has prepared for you!" However, there is no clear evidence for this.

The meaning of "giving life" (احياء) is the initial granting of life in this world, and "causing death" (ميراندن) refers to the end of one's lifespan. The phrase "and to Us is the final return" (إِلَيْنَا الْمَصِيرُ) refers to being brought back to life at the Resurrection.

In fact, the verse points to the fact that just as the initial life and death are in Our hands, the return to life and the rising of the Resurrection are also in Our hands and toward Us.

Then, for further explanation, He says: "Their return to Us is on the Day when the earth breaks away from them, and they emerge in haste" (يَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ الْأَرْضُ عَنْهُمْ سِراعاً). [^1] At the end of the verse, He adds: "That is a gathering easy for Us" (ذلِكَ حَشْرٌ عَلَيْنا يَسِيرٌ). "Hashr" (حشر) means gathering and collecting from every direction.

It is clear that for the God who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth and everything between them, the gathering and resurrection of the dead is a simple task for Him. Fundamentally, "difficult" and "easy" apply to one whose power is limited; for Him whose power is unlimited, everything is equal and easy.

Interestingly, we read in some narrations: "The first person to be brought to life, emerge from the grave, and enter the scene of the Gathering is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and Ali عليه السلام is with him!" [^2]

In the final verse under discussion, which is the last verse of this Surah, He once again consoles and comforts His Prophet in the face of stubborn and persistent opponents, saying: "We know best what they say" (نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَقُولُونَ).

"And you are not one to compel them [to faith] or to drag them toward Islam by force and coercion" (وَ ما أَنْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِجَبَّارٍ).

Your duty is only to convey the message, to call toward the Truth, and to give glad tidings and warnings. "Since it is so, remind by the Quran whoever fears My warning" (فَذَكِّرْ بِالْقُرْآنِ مَنْ يَخافُ وَعِيدِ). [^3] It is mentioned in the Tafsir of Qurtubi that Ibn Abbas says: A group said, "O Messenger of Allah! Warn us and frighten us." Then the above verse was revealed and said:

"So remind by the Quran whoever fears My warning." [^4] This indicates that the Quran is sufficient for warning and awakening believers. Every page of it is a reminder of the Resurrection, its various verses recount the fate of predecessors, and its descriptions of Heavenly blessings, Hellish punishments, and the events occurring on the threshold of the Resurrection and in the Court of Divine Justice are the best advice and counsel for everyone.

Truly, the reminder of that scene where the earth splits apart, and the dust takes on life, dons the garment of existence, begins to move, and emerges from the graves—while terror and anxiety have overwhelmed everyone from head to toe as they are driven toward the Court of Divine Justice—is a soul-shaking scene.

Especially considering that sometimes a single grave, over time, becomes the grave of various people and houses many individuals, some of whom were righteous and some unrighteous, some believers and some disbelievers. As the poet says: Many a grave has become a grave repeatedly—a grave that laughs at the crowding of opposites! And one buried upon the remains of another—over long ages and eras!

O Lord! Place us among those who fear Your "Warning" and take heed from Your Quran. O God! On that Day when terror and anxiety overwhelm everyone, grant us tranquility through Your Mercy.

O Allah! The days of life, whatever they may be, pass quickly; that which is eternal is Your Abode of the Hereafter. Grant us a good end and salvation in the Hereafter.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Qaf. Thursday, 17th of Muharram al-Haram, 1406 AH.

[^1]: "Sira'an" (سراع) is the plural of "Sari'" (سريع), like "Kiram" is the plural of "Karim." Here it is a circumstantial adverb (hal) for the subject of "they emerge" (يخرجون) which is omitted, implied as: "they emerge in haste." Some also consider "Sira'an" a verbal noun (masdar) acting as a circumstantial adverb.

[^2]: Kitab al-Khisal, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 119.

[^3]: Note that "Wa'id" (وعيد) was originally "Wa'idi" (وعيدي); the "Ya" was omitted and the kasra remains as an indicator, and it is the object of "fears" (يخاف).

[^4]: Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6198.
